findings.title: Feature flag gate silently suppresses all exceptions
findings.code_snippet:
except Exception as e:
            logger.debug("Suppressed: %s", e)
findings.description: The feature flag gate in call_llm wraps the entire execution_engine_v2 routing decision in a bare except-Exception that logs at DEBUG level. If the execution engine itself throws a real error (DB connection failure, LLM client crash), the error is silently swallowed and the code falls through to the legacy path. The user never learns their feature-flagged engine failed, and operators will miss it unless DEBUG logging is enabled in production (it typically is not).
findings.suggestion: Log at WARNING level with structured context (agent_id, flag name). Consider only catching the specific import/flag-check exceptions, and letting execution engine errors propagate so they trigger the existing fallback logic or surface to the user.

findings.title: Token limit check silently suppresses DB failures
findings.code_snippet:
except Exception as e:
            logger.debug("Suppressed: %s", e)
findings.description: The agent token limit check (daily/monthly quota) catches all exceptions and logs at DEBUG level. If the database is down, the agent bypasses all token limits and runs unlimited. This is a safety-critical check (cost control) that silently becomes a no-op on infrastructure failure. The agent could consume unlimited tokens without any operator visibility.
findings.suggestion: Fail open with a WARNING log that includes agent_id and error type. Alternatively, fail closed: if the quota check cannot complete, block the request with a clear error message. At minimum, log at WARNING level so operators see token quota enforcement is down.

findings.title: update_llm_model returns generic 500 error hiding root cause
findings.code_snippet:
except Exception as e:
        await db.rollback()
        logger.error("Failed to update LLM model %s: %s", model_id, e)
        raise HTTPException(status_code=500, detail="Failed to update model")
findings.description: The outer catch-all in update_llm_model catches every exception type (including validation errors, constraint violations, and permission errors) and returns a generic 500 with 'Failed to update model'. This makes debugging impossible from the client side and masks errors that should be 400/409 (e.g., duplicate model name, invalid provider). The logger.error call records the real error server-side, but the API consumer gets no useful information.
findings.suggestion: Handle specific exception types: IntegrityError -> 409 with conflict details, ValidationError -> 422, and only use 500 for truly unexpected errors. Include a sanitized error description in the response that helps the client understand what went wrong without leaking stack traces.

findings.title: Task execution launched as fire-and-forget asyncio.create_task
findings.description: execute_task is launched via asyncio.create_task with no error handling, no done callback, and no reference retention. If execute_task raises an exception, it will be silently lost (Python logs 'Task exception was never retrieved' at DEBUG level only). The task could fail without the user or agent knowing. There is also no reference kept to the task object, so it could be garbage collected before completion.
findings.suggestion: At minimum, add a done callback that logs errors: `task = _asyncio.create_task(execute_task(...)); task.add_done_callback(lambda t: t.exception() and logger.error(...))`. Better: store the task reference and handle the result. Best: use a proper task queue (Redis, Celery) for background task execution.

findings.title: Security zone enforcement silently skipped on exception
findings.code_snippet:
except Exception as e:
        logger.debug("Security zone check skipped for agent %s: %s", agent_id, e)
findings.description: The security zone enforcement in execute_tool catches all exceptions and logs at DEBUG level. If the database query for the agent's security_zone fails, the tool executes without any security zone restrictions. A 'public' agent that should only have read-only access could execute sensitive tools (send_email, delete_file) if the DB is temporarily unreachable. This is a security-critical fail-open pattern.
findings.suggestion: Fail closed: if the security zone check fails, block the tool execution and return an error to the agent. Security checks must not be silently bypassed. Use logger.warning at minimum, and default to the most restrictive zone ('public') on failure.

findings.title: WebSocket tool_call persistence silently swallows save failures
findings.code_snippet:
except Exception as _tc_err:
                                print(f"[WS] Failed to save tool_call: {_tc_err}")
findings.description: When a completed tool call fails to persist to the database, the error is caught and printed (not even logged via the logger). The chat history will be missing tool call records, which breaks conversation replay and debugging. Multiple consecutive failures could silently corrupt an entire conversation's tool call history. Uses print() instead of structured logging.
findings.suggestion: Use logger.warning with structured context (agent_id, tool_name, conversation_id). Consider a retry mechanism or at minimum a counter that escalates to ERROR level after N consecutive failures. Replace print() with logger calls as required by CLAUDE.md.

findings.title: ConnectionManager.send_message silently drops messages on WS errors
findings.description: ConnectionManager.send_message iterates over all active connections and sends a message. If any ws.send_json() raises an exception (e.g., connection already closed), the loop aborts and remaining connections never receive the message. There is no try/except around individual sends, no cleanup of dead connections, and no logging of send failures.
findings.suggestion: Wrap each ws.send_json in try/except, log failures, and remove dead connections from the active list. Consider collecting exceptions and reporting at the end rather than failing on the first error.

findings.title: Tavily/Google/Bing API responses parsed without error handling
findings.description: The _search_tavily, _search_google, and _search_bing functions call resp.json() without checking the HTTP status code first and without try/except around the JSON parsing. If the API returns a non-JSON response (HTML error page, 500 with text body, rate limit page), json.JSONDecodeError will propagate up. The outer except in _web_search catches this, but the error message will be unhelpful ('Expecting value: line 1 column 1').
findings.suggestion: Check resp.status_code before parsing JSON. Wrap resp.json() in try/except JSONDecodeError with a descriptive error message that includes the HTTP status code and a preview of the response body.

findings.title: Malformed tool_call history records silently skipped
findings.code_snippet:
except Exception:
                continue  # Skip malformed tool_call records
findings.description: When reconstructing conversation history from persisted tool_call records, any malformed record is silently skipped via a bare except-continue. This means corrupted history data never surfaces as a warning. If a systematic serialization bug produces consistently malformed records, every conversation reconstruction silently loses tool context, degrading agent quality without any diagnostic signal.
findings.suggestion: Log a warning with the message ID and error details so operators can detect and fix systematic data corruption. Even if skipping is the correct behavior, visibility into the skip rate is important.

findings.title: collaboration.list_collaborators returns empty list on agent not found
findings.description: CollaborationService.list_collaborators returns an empty list when the agent_id does not exist in the database. The caller cannot distinguish between 'agent exists but has no collaborators' and 'agent does not exist'. This silent conversion of an error state (missing agent) into a valid-looking response could mask bugs in the calling code.
findings.suggestion: Raise ValueError('Agent not found') consistent with the delegate_task method in the same class. Or use a Result/Either pattern to distinguish 'no collaborators' from 'invalid agent_id'. The API layer can then return 404 vs 200 with empty list appropriately.

positive_observations: Autonomy check in execute_tool correctly fails closed on exception — blocks the operation and returns a clear error message (line 1108-1110)
Audit event writes consistently use a try/except pattern with logger.warning and exc_info=True, which is reasonable for non-critical side effects
The execution engine properly uses reversed middleware order for on_error hooks and re-raises when no middleware handles the error
Agent deletion uses savepoints (begin_nested) for cleanup of related tables, preventing partial cleanup from poisoning the whole transaction
